verdicts.reasoning: The taint analysis incorrectly flagged this as XSS. $active_tab comes from sanitize_key($_GET['rl_active_tab']), which strips everything except lowercase letters, digits, hyphens and underscores. It is then validated against array_key_exists($active_tab, $this->tabs) - so only valid developer-defined tab keys pass through. The user-controlled value is NEVER echoed; it is only used in a ternary comparison ($key === $active_tab ? ' nav-tab-active' : '') to add a CSS class string. What gets echoed is $key (the array key from $this->tabs, a hardcoded developer-defined array) and $data['label'] (also from $this->tabs), both passed through esc_attr() and esc_html() respectively. No XSS possible.

The taint analysis flagged get_option('responsive_lightbox_version') as a taint source leading to unescaped printf output. Two problems with this finding: (1) The page requires 'manage_options' capability (add_dashboard_page with 'manage_options'), so only site administrators can see it. Admins already have full site control. (2) The version option is set by the plugin itself during activation/update (responsive-lightbox.php line 786), not by user input. It stores a developer-controlled version string like '2.2'. Even if an admin could set this value, XSS in an admin-only page accessible exclusively to admins has no meaningful security impact. Additionally, $plugin_version is passed through substr($plugin_version, 0, 3) limiting it to 3 characters. The format string uses esc_html__() which escapes the format string but not the %s argument - this is technically a missing escape, but the complete absence of a user-controlled data path makes this moot.

This AJAX handler (rl-get-gallery-page-content) is registered for both authenticated and unauthenticated users. However: (1) A valid nonce ('rl_nonce') is required via gallery_ajax_verified(), which verifies wp_verify_nonce($_POST['nonce'], 'rl_nonce'). This nonce is generated server-side and embedded in frontend JS (responsive-lightbox.php line 2123). (2) The actual user-controlled parameters are: gallery_id cast to (int), gallery_no cast to (int), preview cast to boolean (=== 'true' comparison), page cast to (int). None of these can carry XSS payload. (3) The gallery_shortcode() function pulls all output from database-stored gallery metadata, and escapes everything with esc_attr() throughout (class-galleries.php lines 482-484, 511, 521, 536). The output of do_shortcode() also passes through add_lightbox() which uses wp_kses_post() and esc_url(). No unescaped user-controlled data flows to output.

This is not SSRF. The URL in wp_remote_post() is hardcoded to 'https://www.dfactory.co/wp-json/api/v1/forms/' - user-controlled input only affects the POST body parameters ('option' and 'other'), not the URL. The user cannot control the host or protocol. Per the SSRF exclusion rule: 'SSRF is only a concern if it can control the host or protocol.' Additionally, the handler requires 'install_plugins' capability and a valid nonce, so only privileged users can trigger this. The $other value is sanitized with sanitize_text_field() before inclusion in the POST body. This is a legitimate plugin deactivation feedback form.

notes: Reviewed all 4 taint flows from the automated analysis plus conducted manual review of: all AJAX handlers (authenticated and unauthenticated), settings validation/sanitization logic, gallery shortcode rendering pipeline, remote library image upload handler, folder management AJAX handlers, and file system operations. The plugin demonstrates generally good security hygiene: consistent use of esc_attr/esc_html/esc_url for output, wp_kses_post for HTML content, sanitize_key/sanitize_text_field for input, nonce verification on all state-changing operations, and capability checks on all admin functions. No exploitable vulnerabilities were identified.
